issues.comment: `parseRefreshTokenResponse` returns the full `safeParse()` result object (`{ success: true, data: { ... } }`) instead of `.data`. In Google Calendar, `key` gets stored in the database wrapped in the SafeParseResult. The next time the credential is loaded and parsed via `googleCredentialSchema.parse(credential.key)`, it will fail because the stored shape is `{ success, data: {...} }` instead of `{ access_token, refresh_token, ... }`. This corrupts Google Calendar credentials for all users on every token refresh.

Invalid Zod schema: `z.string()` returns a `ZodString` object, and calling `.toString()` on it uses `Object.prototype.toString()`, which returns `"[object Object]"`. Both computed property keys resolve to the same string `"[object Object]"`, so the second overwrites the first. The resulting schema is effectively `z.object({ access_token: z.string(), "[object Object]": z.unknown().optional() })`. This does not create a schema that validates "any string key maps to a number" as intended, so it won't enforce the presence of an expiry field.

zoho-bigin passes `credentialId` (which is `credential.id`, the credential's database row ID) instead of `credential.userId` to `refreshOAuthTokens` as the `userId` parameter. Every other integration correctly passes `credential.userId`. When credential sharing is enabled, this sends the wrong user ID to the external endpoint, causing token refresh to fail or refresh tokens for the wrong user.
